volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 4.2.896b - Section 5.2.998)

sections.label: Section 4.2.896b
sections.original: Quia illas emit in Anactorio parvolas
sections.translation: Because he bought them at Anactorium , when little children , of a Sicilian pirate .

sections.label: Section 4.2.897b
sections.original: Quanti ?
sections.translation: For how much ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.897c
sections.original: Duodeviginti minis ,
sections.translation: For eighteen minae .

sections.label: Section 4.2.898
sections.original: duas illas et Giddenenem nutricem earum tertiam .
sections.translation: And their nurse for the third .

sections.label: Section 4.2.899
sections.original: et ille qui eas vendebat dixit se furtivas vendere :
sections.translation: He , too , who sold them told him that he was selling persons who had been kidnapped :

sections.label: Section 4.2.900
sections.original: ingenuas Carthagine aibat esse .
sections.translation: he said that they were free-born , and from Carthage .

sections.label: Section 4.2.900b
sections.original: Di vostram fidem ,
sections.translation: Ye Gods , by our hopes in you ! you mention a most interesting matter ; for my master Agorastocles was born in the same place ; he was stolen thence when about six years old ; after that , the person who stole him brought him here and sold him to my master ; that person adopted him as heir to his wealth , when he departed this life .

sections.label: Section 4.2.905
sections.original: Omnia memoras quo id facilius fiat : manu eas adserat
sections.translation: You mention everything that can render it the more easy ; let him assert their freedom , his own countrywomen , in an action on their freedom .

sections.label: Section 4.2.906b
sections.original: Tacitus tace modo .
sections.translation: Only do keep silence and hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 4.2.907
sections.original: Profecto ad incitas lenonem rediget , si eas abduxerit .
sections.translation: He certainly will bring the Procurer to a backgammon , if he gets them away .

sections.label: Section 4.2.908
sections.original: Quin prius disperibit faxo , quam unam calcem civerit .
sections.translation: Nay but , I ’ ll cause him to be ruined before he moves one foot [*] ; ’ tis so contrived already .

sections.label: Section 4.2.909b
sections.original: Ita di faxint , ne apud lenonem hunc serviam .
sections.translation: May the Gods grant it so , that I don ’ t continue the slave of this Procurer .

sections.label: Section 4.2.910
sections.original: Hercle qui meus conlibertus faxo eris , si di volent .
sections.translation: On the contrary , upon my faith , I ’ ll cause you to be a free man with myself , if the Gods are willing .

sections.label: Section 4.2.911
sections.original: Ita di faxint . numquid aliud me morare , Milphio ?
sections.translation: May the Gods grant it so ! Do you detain me for anything else , Milphio ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.912
sections.original: Valeas beneque ut tibi sit .
sections.translation: Fare you well , and may happiness attend you .

sections.label: Section 4.2.912b
sections.original: Pol istuc tibi et tuost ero in manu .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , that lies in the power of yourself and your master . Farewell , and mind that these things have been told in secrecy .

sections.label: Section 4.2.913b
sections.original: Non dictumst . vale .
sections.translation: This has not been mentioned even . Farewell .

sections.label: Section 4.2.914
sections.original: At enim nihil est , nisi dum calet hoc agitur .
sections.translation: But really it ’ s of no use , unless this is done while it is warm .

sections.label: Section 4.2.914b
sections.original: Lepidu'slepidus es , quom mones .
sections.translation: You are right in your advice ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.915
sections.original: et ita hoc fiet .
sections.translation: and so it shall be done .

sections.label: Section 4.2.915b
sections.original: Proba materies data est , si probum adhibes fabrum .
sections.translation: There ’ s excellent material , if you provide an excellent workman .

sections.label: Section 4.2.916
sections.original: Potin ut taceas ?
sections.translation: Can ’ t you hold your tongue ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.916b
sections.original: Taceo atque abeo .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll hold my tongue and be off .

sections.label: Section 4.2.916c
sections.original: Mihi commoditatem creas .
sections.translation: A grand opportunity you ’ ve made for me . ( SYNCERASTUS goes into the house of LYCUS .) He ’ s gone from here . The immortal Gods do will my master to be preserved , and this Procurer utterly ruined ; a mischief so great is impending upon him . Is it not the fact , before one weapon has been launched , then another presses upon him ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.920
sections.original: ibo intro , haec ut meo ero memorem . nam huc si ante aedes evocem ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in-doors , that I may recount these matters to my master . For if I were to call him out hither before the house , and , what you ’ ve ( to the AUDIENCE ) just heard , if I were now here to repeat the same , it would be folly . I ’ d rather in-doors be an annoyance to my master singly , than be so here to all of you . Immortal Gods , what misfortunes , what great calamities do this day await this Procurer . But now there ’ s no reason why I should delay .

sections.label: Section 4.2.925
sections.original: ita negotium institutumst , non datur cessatio ;
sections.translation: This business is resolved upon ; no pausing is allowed ; for both this must be cleverly managed , which has just now been entrusted to me , and that plan as well which was formed at home must be attended to . If there ’ s any delay , he who sends me a heavy mischance will be acting rightly . Now I ’ ll off in-doors ; until my master comes from the Forum , I ’ ll wait at home .

sections.label: Section 5.1.930
sections.original: Yth alonim ualonuth sicorathi symacom syth
sections.translation: Hyth alonim [*] vualonuth sicorathi si ma com sith , Chi mach chumyth mumys tyal mictibariim ischi , Lipho canet luth bynuthi ad aedin bynuthii . Birnarob syllo homalonin uby misyrthoho Bythym mothym noctothii velech Antidasmachon .

sections.label: Section 5.1.935
sections.original: ys sidobrim chi fel yth chyl is chon chen liful
sections.translation: Yssidele berim thyfel yth chylys chon , tern , lyphul Uth bynim ysdibut thinno cuth ru Agorastocles Ythe manet ihy ⋯ chyrsae lycoch sith naso Byuni id chil luhili gerbylim lasibit thym Bodyalyth herayn nyn nuys lym moncoth lusim .

sections.label: Section 5.1.940
sections.original: Ythalonimualoniuthsicorathi i sthymhim i hymacomsyth
sections.translation: [ Exalonim volanus succuratim mistim Atticum esse Concubitum a bello cutim beant lalacant chona Enus es huiec silec panesse Athidamascon Alem ⋯ induberte felono ⋯ buthume Celtum comucro lueni , at enim avoso uber Bent hyach Aristoclem et se te aneche nasoctelia Elicos alemus [ in ] duberter mi comps vespiti Aodeanee lictor bodes jussum limnicolus .]

sections.label: Section 5.2.961
sections.original: Ain tú tibi dixe Syncerastum , Milphio ,
sections.translation: Do you say , Milphio , that Syncerastus told you that both of these women were freeborn , and stolen away from Carthage ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.963b
sections.original: Aio , et , si frugi esse vis ,
sections.translation: I do say so ; and if you were willing to act wisely , you ’ d at once assert their liberty by an action on their freedom .

sections.label: Section 5.2.965
sections.original: nam tuom flagitiumst tuas te popularis pati
sections.translation: For it ’ s a disgrace to you for you to allow your own country-people to be slaves before your eyes , who were free women at home .

sections.label: Section 5.2.967
sections.original: Pro di immortales , opsecro vostram fidem ,
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , I do entreat your aid ! What speech is this that my ears devour ! Surely the words of these persons are made of chalk ;

sections.label: Section 5.2.970
sections.original: ut mi apsterserunt omnem sorditudinem .
sections.translation: how have they cleansed away all the dark spots of woe from me !

sections.label: Section 5.2.971
sections.original: Si ad eam rem testis habeam , faciam quod iubes .
sections.translation: If you ’ ve got witnesses of this matter , I ’ ll do as you bid me .

sections.label: Section 5.2.972
sections.original: Quid tu mihi testis ? quin tu insistis fortiter ?
sections.translation: Why speak you to me about witnesses ? Why don ’ t you stoutly insist upon it ? Some way or other , Fortune will be your assistant .

sections.label: Section 5.2.974
sections.original: Incipere multost quam impetrare facilius .
sections.translation: It ’ s much more easy to begin a thing than to bring it about .

sections.label: Section 5.2.975
sections.original: Sed quae illaec avis est , quae huc cum tunicis advenit ?
sections.translation: But what bird is this [*] , pray , that ’ s coming hither with the tunic on ? Is he from the baths [*] , I wonder , enveloped in his cloak ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.977
sections.original: Facies quidem edepol Punicast .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , the countenance is surely Carthaginian .

sections.label: Section 5.2.977b
sections.original: Guggast homo .
sections.translation: The man ’ s a Gugga [*] . I ’ faith , he certainly has got some ancient and antiquated servants .

sections.label: Section 5.2.979
sections.original: Qui scis ?
sections.translation: How do you know ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.979b
sections.original: Viden homines sarcinatos consequi ?
sections.translation: Don ’ t you see the fellows following , loaded with luggage ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.980
sections.original: † atque ut opinor digitos in manibus non habent .
sections.translation: And , as I fancy , they ’ ve got no fingers on their hands .

sections.label: Section 5.2.981
sections.original: Quid iam ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.981b
sections.original: Quia incedunt cum anulatis auribus .
sections.translation: Why , because they go with their rings in their ears [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.2.982
sections.original: Adibo hosce atque áppellabo Punice .
sections.translation: I ’ ll approach them , and address them in the Punic language : if they answer , I ’ ll continue to speak in the Punic tongue if not , then I ’ ll adapt my language to their usage .

sections.label: Section 5.2.985
sections.original: Quid ais tu ? écquid commeministi Punice ?
sections.translation: How say you , do you still remember anything of the Punic language ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.986
sections.original: Nihil edepol . nam qui scire potui , dic mihi ,
sections.translation: Nothing at all , i ’ faith ; for tell me , how could I know , who was but six years old when I was stolen away from Carthage ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.988
sections.original: Pro di immortales , plurumi ad illum modum
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods ! very many freeborn children have been lost from Carthage after this manner .

sections.label: Section 5.2.990
sections.original: Quid ais tu ?
sections.translation: How say you ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.990b
sections.original: Quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.990c
sections.original: Vin appellem hunc Punice ?
sections.translation: Should you like me to address this person in the Punic tongue ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.991
sections.original: An scis ?
sections.translation: Do you understand it ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.991b
sections.original: Nullus me est hodie Poenus Poenior .
sections.translation: No Punic man this day is a better Punic than I .

sections.label: Section 5.2.992
sections.original: Adi atque appella , quid velit , quid venerit ,
sections.translation: Go and address him , as to what he wants , why he ’ s come , who he is , of what country , and whence he comes . Don ’ t be sparing of your questions .

sections.label: Section 5.2.994
sections.original: Avo . quoiates estis aut quo ex oppido ?
sections.translation: Avo ! [*] Of what country are you , or from what city ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.995
sections.original: Anno byn mytthymballe udradait annech .
sections.translation: Hanno Muthumballe bachaëdreanech .

sections.label: Section 5.2.996
sections.original: Quid ait ?
sections.translation: What does he say ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.996b
sections.original: Hannonem se esse ait Carthagine ,
sections.translation: He says that he is Hanno from Carthage , a Carthaginian , son of Muthumbal .

sections.label: Section 5.2.998
sections.original: Avo .
sections.translation: Avo !
